content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array of integers where every element appears exactly twice, except for one unique element that appears only once. Chef wants to find this single unique integer. To help Chef, you need to implement an efficient solution that runs in linear time and uses only constant extra space. Help Chef identify the number that stands out from the duplicates so he can complete his task successfully.

Function Declaration

**Function Name**



findSingleNumberfindSingleNumberfindSingleNumber - This function identifies the unique integer in a list where every other integer appears twice.



**Parameters**



numbersnumbersnumbers ( Array(int)Array(int)Array(int) ): A reference to a vector of integers containing the input numbers.
The list contains integers where exactly one integer appears once, and all others appear twice.



**Return Value**



Returns an intintint representing the single number that appears only once in the input list.
The output is a single integer value that does not have a duplicate in the list.


Constraints
1â‰¤Tâ‰¤1051 \leq T \leq 10^51â‰¤Tâ‰¤105
âˆ’3Ã—104â‰¤numbers[i]â‰¤3Ã—104-3 \times 10^4 \leq numbers[i] \leq 3 \times 10^4âˆ’3Ã—104â‰¤numbers[i]â‰¤3Ã—104
Exactly one element appears once; all others appear twice.
Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT â€” the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The First line contains NNN integers â€” the elements of Chef's array.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing the integer that appears only once in Chef's array.
constraints: Exactly one element appears once; all others appear twice.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N spaceâ€‘separated integers. Exactly one integer appears once, all others appear exactly twice.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the integer that appears only once.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, 7 and 3 each appear twice, so XOR of all numbers is 0. In the second test case, 10, 14, and 18 appear twice, leaving 22 as the unique element.
First case: only -10000 exists. Second case: 0 cancels out, leaving -30000. Third case: all numbers appear twice, so XOR is 0.
All numbers except 7000 appear twice; XOR leaves 7000.

solution.approach: Traverse the array while maintaining a variable x initialized to 0. For each element a, set x = x ^ a. Because a ^ a = 0 and 0 ^ b = b, all numbers that appear twice cancel out, leaving x equal to the unique element. This runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the size of the array. Overall O(T * N).

code.Python3:
import sys

def find_single(nums):
    x = 0
    for v in nums:
        x ^= v
    return x

data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
it = iter(data)
T = int(next(it))
out_lines = []
for _ in range(T):
    N = int(next(it))
    nums = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
    out_lines.append(str(find_single(nums)))
print('\n'.join(out_lines))
